1: A direct revelation: “man born of woman is short-lived and full of turmoil.” Paul uses this exact phrase — “born of woman” (genomenon ek gynaikos) — for Christ’s incarnation in Galatians 4:4–5: “God sent his Son, born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those under the law.” Christ is the “man born of woman” who, being short-lived and full of turmoil in his humanity, redeems precisely the human condition Job describes — not by escaping it but by entering and bearing it to its depths.
2: Man comes up like a flower that wilts and passes like a shadow that does not endure — the poetic statement of human transience that Christ entered fully. Hebrews 2:14–15: “since the children have flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death.” Christ did not bypass the withering and the shadow-passing; he went through it — and by going through it he reversed the outcome, flowering again in resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20).
3: “Yet on such a creature do you fix your eyes, and bring him into court before you?” Job’s amazement that God fixes his eyes on frail humanity echoes Psalms 8:4: “what is man that you are mindful of him, the son of man that you care for him?” Hebrews 2:6–9 applies Ps 8 to Christ, who “for a little while was made lower than the angels” specifically to identify with this fragile creature — but “now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death.” God’s fixed gaze on frail humanity finds its fullest expression in the incarnation.
4: “Who can bring something pure from something impure? No one can.” This impossibility statement is the theological problem that Christ’s virgin birth and sinless humanity address. He was “born of woman” (v1; Galatians 4:4) yet was the pure from the impure lineage: “without sin” (Hebrews 4:15; 1 Peter 2:22). The one born of sinful human stock who was yet without sin is the necessary condition of the incarnation’s redemptive purpose — only the pure can make the impure pure (1 John 1:7).
5: “Since his days are fixed, the number of his months determined by you, the limit set that he cannot cross.” Christ came “in the fullness of time” (Galatians 4:4) — the divinely fixed moment. The “hour” of Christ’s death was specifically predetermined: “no one could seize him, because his hour had not yet come” (John 7:30; Job 8:20); “now the Son of Man is glorified” (John 13:31 when the hour arrives). The fixed number of his months was precisely three decades of earthly life before the appointed hour of his death.
6: “Look away from him so he can rest, until he has completed his time like a hired worker.” Christ is the one who calls the weary to rest: “come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest” (Matthew 11:28–30). He himself rested in the tomb on the Sabbath after completing the work of redemption — “it is finished” (John 19:30; Hebrews 4:10: “anyone who enters God’s rest also rests from their works, just as God did from his”). The completion of the hired servant’s time anticipates the completed work.
7: A type: “there is hope for a tree: if it is cut down, it will sprout again, and its new growth will not stop.” This tree-hope is the structural type underlying Isaiah 11:1: “a shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit.” The Davidic dynasty was cut down in exile; Christ is the shoot from the stump. The cross appears to cut down the tree finally; the resurrection is the sprout from the cut stump. 1 Corinthians 15:37: “what you sow does not come to life unless it dies” — the grain of wheat must fall into the ground (John 12:24).
8: “Though its root grows old in the earth and its stump dies in the soil.” The dying of the root in the earth is the death and burial stage of the type: the Tree of Jesse apparently extinguished, the Davidic line apparently ended. The three days in the tomb correspond to the seed in the ground: “unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed; but if it dies, it produces many seeds” (John 12:24). Death is not the end of the tree — it is the necessary precondition of its renewal.
9: A direct type: “yet at the smell of water it will bud and put out branches like a new plant.” The resurrection of the cut tree at the touch of water is the resurrection type that Job 14 offers the OT. The resurrection of Christ is the fulfillment: “Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep” (1 Corinthians 15:20). The “new plant” that sprouts from the old stump is the new creation that emerges from Christ’s death — “if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come” (2 Corinthians 5:17).
10: “But man dies and lies helpless; he breathes his last — and where is he?” This is the theological problem that the entire NT answers. Christ’s resurrection is the definitive response to “where is he?” — the empty tomb is the answer: he is not here, he is risen (Luke 24:6). John 11:25: “I am the resurrection and the life; the one who believes in me will live, even though they die.” The “where is he?” of death becomes the “he is here” of the resurrection appearances (John 20:19–29).
11: As water vanishes from a lake and a river wastes away and dries up — so Job describes the irreversible disappearance of the dead. Christ reversed irreversibility: “I am the resurrection and the life; whoever lives by believing in me will never die” (John 11:26). The water that vanishes from the lake is the same element Christ uses to describe eternal life: “a spring of water welling up to eternal life” (John 4:14) — the water that does not vanish but rises eternally.
12: “So man lies down and does not rise; until the heavens are no more, they will not wake or be roused from their sleep.” Job’s “until the heavens are no more” is the eschatological limit: the moment when the heavens pass away. Revelation 21:1: “I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away.” Christ is the one who brings this “until” to pass and raises the dead in connection with it (1 Thessalonians 4:16: “the dead in Christ will rise first”; 1 Corinthians 15:23–26).
13: “O that you would hide me in Sheol until your anger passes, set a fixed time for me, and then remember me.” Job’s longing to be hidden in Sheol and then remembered is the deepest OT prayer for post-death divine remembrance. Christ entered Sheol — “you will not abandon me to the realm of the dead” (Acts 2:27, quoting Psalms 16:10); “he was put to death in the body but made alive in the Spirit” (1 Peter 3:18). He was remembered by the Father and raised — the enacted answer to Job’s desperate wish.
14: A direct revelation: “if a man dies, will he live again? Through all my days of hard service I will wait until my release comes.” This is the central question of the book of Job and of all human existence. Christ’s resurrection is the definitive answer. 1 Corinthians 15:12–22: “if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile” — and since Christ has been raised, the answer to Job’s “will he live again?” is an unreserved yes. The “release” Job waits for is the resurrection release that Christ obtained first and now shares (Hebrews 2:14–15).
15: A direct revelation: “you will call out to me, and I will answer; you will long for the work of your hands.” The divine longing for the creature — “you will long for the work of your hands” — is one of the most tender statements of divine love in Job. It finds its ultimate expression in the resurrection: Jesus calling Mary’s name (“Mary!” John 20:16) and her turning to him is the enacted fulfillment — the Creator calling, the creature answering. The resurrection of the dead is God longing for and reclaiming the work of his hands.
16: “But now you count my every step and watch over my sin without fail.” The divine surveillance that condemns is what Christ bore for us: “the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all” (Isaiah 53:6). His steps were counted, his path was watched — and declared perfectly righteous. For those in Christ, the divine surveillance no longer condemns because he was “delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification” (Romans 4:25). The count of every step now yields acquittal, not condemnation.
17: “My transgression is sealed in a bag; you have stitched up my guilt.” The sin sealed in a bag is the image of the permanent record held against the accused. Christ unsealed and emptied that bag: “he forgave us all our sins, having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness, which stood against us and condemned us; he has taken it away, nailing it to the cross” (Colossians 2:13–14). The bag that was sealed is now empty; the stitched guilt is now torn open and forgiven.
18: A mountain eroding comes to nothing; a rock dislodged from its place — all human stability eventually crumbles. Christ is the Rock that does not crumble: 1 Corinthians 10:4 (“they drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ”); Matthew 16:18 (“on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it”). The erosion that Job describes as characteristic of all earthly things is precisely what Christ does not undergo: “he who is built on this rock will not fall” (Matthew 7:25).
19: “Water wears down the stones; floods wash away the soil of the earth — and so you wash away the hope of man.” Job’s conclusion that all human hope is eroded by the waters of death and time is the verdict that Christ’s resurrection overturns. He is “the hope of glory” (Colossians 1:27: “Christ in you, the hope of glory”). The hope of man is not washed away in Christ — rather, he provides an “anchor for the soul, firm and secure” (Hebrews 6:19–20) that no flood of suffering or death can erode.
20: “You overpower him completely and he is gone; you alter his countenance and send him away.” Death’s overpowering is the condition Christ entered and reversed. He was overpowered by death (Mark 15:37: “and with a loud cry, Jesus breathed his last”); his countenance was altered beyond recognition (Isaiah 52:14: “his appearance was so disfigured beyond that of any human being”). But then: “I am the Living One; I was dead, and now look, I am alive for ever and ever! And I hold the keys of death and Hades” (Revelation 1:18). He was overpowered and he overpowered death.
21: “His sons rise to honor but he does not know it; they are brought low but he is not aware.” Job describes the isolation of the dead from their descendants. Christ’s resurrection broke this isolation: as the “firstborn among many brothers” (Romans 8:29) he lives and participates in the honor of his brothers and sisters who believe. Hebrews 2:11–12: “he is not ashamed to call them brothers and sisters” — the risen Christ is aware of and invested in the honor and lowliness of those who are his, unlike the dead in Sheol who know nothing.
22: “He feels only the pain within him and mourns only over himself.” The dead man’s self-absorbed pain is the final picture of death’s isolation — the ultimate loneliness. Christ’s death was the radical opposite: even on the cross he turned outward (“Father, forgive them,” Luke 23:34; “today you will be with me in paradise,” Luke 23:43; caring for his mother, John 19:26–27). His resurrection ends the isolation of death and opens the community of the new creation. The self-mourning of Job 14 is the condition Christ entered; the self-giving of the cross is the condition he enacted; the community of the resurrection is the condition he opens.
